worth_it_explanation: Reported revenue and fill are the numbers a publisher bills against, plans against, and trusts when judging which demand to keep. When the SpringServe console disagrees with the payments that actually arrive, or with the numbers a demand partner reports, the gap erodes that trust and can hide a real shortfall. This check reads the configuration and the reconciliation a publisher already has rather than rebuilding anything, so it costs review time, and the finding points to a specific discrepancy a publisher can chase down with its partners.
intro: SpringServe is a video and Connected TV (CTV) ad server that reports the revenue, fill, and delivery a publisher earns across direct-sold and programmatic demand. This subcategory is about whether those reported numbers can be trusted, which rests on two things. The first is reconciliation, whether the revenue and fill SpringServe reports line up with the payments and remittances that actually arrive from demand partners. The second is comparability, whether report dimensions, timezones, and counting methods are set so the numbers can be compared like for like across SpringServe and the systems a publisher reconciles against. These checks confirm that the reported numbers are anchored to money received and that the comparison is being made on a common basis.

audit_checks.title: Reported revenue and fill are not reconciled against partner remittances
audit_checks.impact_rationale: SpringServe reports the revenue a publisher should expect, but the money that arrives comes from each demand partner on its own terms after the partner applies its own counting, invalid-traffic removal, and discrepancy rules. When the reported number is never checked against the remittance, a standing gap between the two goes unnoticed, so a publisher may plan and bill against revenue it will not collect or fail to chase a partner that is underpaying.

educate.base:
A video and Connected TV (CTV) ad server such as SpringServe reports the revenue and fill it records as ads are requested and rendered. That reported revenue is an estimate of what a publisher has earned, built from the prices and impression counts SpringServe sees. The money that actually arrives is a separate number. Each demand partner pays on its own remittance, after applying its own impression counting, its own removal of invalid traffic, and its own discrepancy allowance. The two numbers describe the same delivery, so they should track closely, but they are produced by different systems counting in different ways.

A discrepancy between the two is expected to some degree. The Media Rating Council (MRC) guidance for digital video and Connected TV requires a client-initiated count and the filtering of invalid traffic before an impression qualifies, and a buy-side or partner-side count that applies stricter rendering or viewability criteria will land lower than a server-side served count. A small, stable gap is the normal cost of two systems measuring independently. The audit concern is a gap that is large, that drifts over time, or that nobody is watching because that is where a real shortfall hides.

Reconciliation is the practice of comparing SpringServe's reported revenue and fill for a period against the remittances and reports the demand partners actually send for that same period, partner by partner. When the comparison is run on a schedule, an unexpected gap surfaces while it can still be raised with the partner. When it is never run, the reported number stands unchallenged and a publisher plans, forecasts, and bills against revenue it may not collect.

A few signs point to revenue and fill that are not being reconciled:
  • No regular comparison exists between SpringServe's reported revenue and the partner remittances for the same period
  • The publisher cannot state the typical discrepancy percentage for each major demand partner
  • A standing gap between reported and paid revenue has gone unexplained or unraised with the partner
  • Reconciliation, where it happens, is a once-a-year scramble at audit time rather than a periodic check
You may want to confirm that reported revenue and fill are compared against partner remittances on a regular basis. One approach is to reconcile each major partner monthly, record the typical discrepancy for each, and treat a gap that moves outside its usual range as something to raise with the partner.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster running ad-supported video, revenue often flows from a mix of direct-sold insertion orders and several programmatic partners, each remitting on its own cycle. You may want to reconcile the direct-sold revenue against the signed insertion orders and reconcile each programmatic partner against its own remittance, so a gap can be traced to the specific source rather than lost in a blended total.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-led property running video against shoppable or product content, sponsored video placements may be sold directly while filler inventory runs programmatically. You may want to reconcile the sponsored, directly-sold revenue against the agreed rate separately from the programmatic remittances since the sponsored inventory usually carries the higher price and a discrepancy there costs more.

learn_more.source_title: MRC Standards and Guidelines (Digital Video Impression Measurement Guidelines)
Server-side Ad Insertion and OTT Guidance

investigate.intro: Review whether a regular comparison exists between SpringServe's reported revenue and fill and the remittances each demand partner sends for the same period, and record the typical discrepancy for each major partner.

springserve_console.label: SpringServe console
steps: Open the reporting section and build a revenue report for a closed period, broken out by demand partner or demand tag, with revenue, impressions, and fill rate.
Record SpringServe's reported revenue and fill for each major demand partner for that period.
Ask the publisher for the remittance or partner report covering the same period and partner, and record the revenue and impression counts the partner actually reported.
Compare the two per partner, record the discrepancy as a percentage, and note whether that gap is within the range the publisher considers normal for that partner.

partner_statements.label: Demand partner statements
steps: Collect the remittance statements and any partner-side reports for the period being reconciled.
Confirm each statement covers the same date range and timezone as the SpringServe report it is being compared against.
Note any partner for which no statement or report is available since that revenue cannot be reconciled and stands on the SpringServe number alone.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per major demand partner, SpringServe's reported revenue and fill for a closed period alongside the revenue and counts the partner remitted for the same period, and the resulting discrepancy. A setup where each major partner is reconciled on a regular schedule, the typical discrepancy is known, and gaps outside the usual range are raised passes. A setup where reported revenue is never compared against remittances, or where a standing gap is unexplained, is a fail recorded with the partners affected and the size of the gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record SpringServe's reported revenue and fill against each partner's remittance for the same period above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not regularly reconcile the revenue and fill SpringServe reports against the remittances its demand partners actually pay. SpringServe reports an estimate of earned revenue from the impressions it counts, while each partner pays after applying its own counting and invalid-traffic rules, so a gap between the two is expected, but a gap that is large, drifting, or unwatched can hide revenue {{site_name}} will not collect. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling each major demand partner against its remittance for the same closed period on a regular schedule, recording the typical discrepancy for each, and raising any gap that moves outside its usual range with the partner. After the practice is in place, the reported numbers can be trusted because they are anchored to the money received.

audit_checks.title: Report dimensions and timezones are not aligned for comparison across systems
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Even when SpringServe and a partner are counting the same delivery, a report built on a different timezone, date boundary, or impression event than the system it is compared against produces a gap that is an artifact of the report settings, not a real discrepancy. That false gap sends a publisher chasing a problem that does not exist and can mask a real one because the noise hides the signal.

educate.base:
Two reports can describe the same ad delivery and still disagree because of how each report was built rather than what each system counted. The most common cause is the timezone and the date boundary. A SpringServe report run in one timezone and a partner report run in another will split the same day differently, so the impressions and revenue that fall on each calendar date will not match even when the totals over a long enough window do. A day-by-day comparison across a timezone gap shows a discrepancy that is purely an artifact of where each system draws the line between one day and the next.

The counting event is the second cause. A served-impression count fires when the ad is sent to the player, while a rendered or viewable count fires later, after the ad begins to play or meets a viewability threshold. The Media Rating Council (MRC) defines these as different events, and a viewable video impression requires that at least 50 percent of the ad's pixels are in view for a minimum of two continuous seconds. A report counting served impressions will always sit higher than one counting rendered or viewable impressions for the same delivery, so comparing across the two measures a definition difference, not an error.

Report dimensions are the third cause. If one report groups by demand partner and another groups by deal or by supply tag, or if one applies a date filter the other does not, the two are not describing the same slice of inventory. A comparison across mismatched dimensions produces a number that does not mean what it appears to mean.

A few signs point to dimensions and timezones that are not aligned for comparison:
  • SpringServe reports and partner reports are run in different timezones without that difference being accounted for in the comparison
  • A served-impression count is compared directly against a rendered or viewable count as though they should match
  • The two reports group by different dimensions or apply different date filters, so they cover different slices of inventory
  • Day-level discrepancies appear that shrink or vanish when the comparison is widened to a full week or month
You may want to confirm that any report used for reconciliation or partner comparison is set to the same timezone, the same counting event, and the same dimensions as the system it is compared against. One approach is to fix a single reconciliation timezone, compare served against served and viewable against viewable, and widen the window to a full settlement period before reading the gap.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a streaming service or broadcaster, ad pods run across audiences in several timezones, and server-side ad insertion can record an impression at a different point than a client-side player would. You may want to standardize on one reporting timezone for reconciliation and confirm whether the count is server-recorded or client-confirmed, so a comparison against a partner reflects delivery rather than a clock or counting difference.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-led property running video against product content, reporting may need to align with a finance or order-period calendar rather than the ad server's default day. You may want to set the SpringServe report to the same period boundary the finance team reconciles against, so the ad revenue lines up with the accounting period instead of splitting across it.

learn_more.source_title: MRC Standards and Guidelines (Digital Video Impression Measurement Guidelines)

investigate.intro: Review the timezone, the counting event, the date filters, and the grouping dimensions on the reports used for reconciliation, and confirm they match the system each report is compared against.

springserve_console.label: SpringServe console
steps: Open a report used for reconciliation or partner comparison and record its timezone setting, its date range, and the dimensions it groups by.
Confirm whether the impressions it reports are served, rendered, or viewable counts.
Compare those settings against the partner report or the system the SpringServe report is reconciled against, and note any timezone, counting-event, date-filter, or dimension that does not match.
Rebuild the comparison with matched settings, or widen it to a full settlement period, and note whether a day-level gap shrinks once the settings are aligned.

partner_statements.label: Demand partner statements
steps: Record the timezone, the date boundary, and the impression event (e.g., served, rendered, or viewable) each partner statement is reported on.
Note any partner whose reporting basis differs from the SpringServe report it is compared against since that difference accounts for part of any gap before a real discrepancy is considered.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the timezone, counting event, date filter, and grouping dimensions of each reconciliation report alongside the corresponding settings of the system it is compared against. A setup where the compared reports share a timezone, compare like counting events, and group on the same dimensions passes. A setup where reports are compared across mismatched timezones, counting events, or dimensions, producing gaps that are artifacts of the settings, is a fail recorded with the specific mismatch.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the timezone, counting event, and dimensions of each report against those of the system it is compared to above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the reports {{site_name}} uses for reconciliation are not aligned with the systems they are compared against on timezone, counting event, or grouping dimensions. A SpringServe report run on a different timezone, counting served impressions against a partner's rendered or viewable count, or grouped on a different dimension produces a gap that is an artifact of the report settings rather than a real discrepancy, which sends effort toward a problem that does not exist and can mask one that does. {{voice_recommend}} fixing a single reconciliation timezone, comparing served against served and viewable against viewable, matching the grouping dimensions, and widening the comparison to a full settlement period before reading the gap. After the settings are aligned, any remaining gap reflects a genuine difference worth raising with the partner.
